explanation: The natural language states that the system must perform a self-test, ensuring both the system and its accessories are fully functional and that alarms work. Among the atomic propositions, 'selfTestPass' indicates all self-test steps passed successfully, and 'testAlarmsPass' indicates local alarms test pass (success). To capture the requirement that the system and alarms must both be checked and work, the property should require that whenever SelfTest Mode is active (SelfTestMode), eventually both the self-test as a whole and the alarm tests are passed. This can be expressed by: globally, if SelfTestMode is active, then eventually both selfTestPass and testAlarmsPass must hold. This property focuses on the logical combination of the relevant atomic propositions and does not use numerical comparisons.

The natural language input requires that the system executes a self-test procedure ensuring both the system and its accessories are fully functional and that the alarms work. Among the available atomic propositions, 'selfTestPass' (indicating all self-test steps passed successfully) and 'testAlarmsPass' (indicating local alarms test pass) are most closely related to this requirement. The property should globally require that if the system is in SelfTestMode, eventually both the self-test and the alarm test pass. This is captured by: G (SelfTestMode -> (F selfTestPass & F testAlarmsPass)). This formula guarantees that whenever the system enters SelfTestMode, it will, at some future point, complete both the general self-test procedure and confirm that the alarms work.

The input natural language states that the system must have a self-test procedure that ensures both the system (and its accessories) are fully functional and the alarms work. Among the atomic propositions, 'selfTestPass' specifically indicates a state when all self-test steps are passed successfully, while 'testAlarmsPass' indicates the local alarms test has passed. Thus, to express that the system (and accessories) is fully functional and the alarms work, we require that globally, if the self-test passes, then also the alarms test passes. This is captured by the property: 'G(selfTestPass -> testAlarmsPass)', meaning that it must always be the case that whenever all self-tests are passed, the alarm test must also have passed, thus covering both requirements in the statement.

The requirement states that the system must have a self-test procedure that ensures both the system and its accessories are fully functional and that the alarms work. The most direct representation, given the available atomic propositions, is to require that whenever the system is in SelfTestMode, eventually the selfTestPass and the testAlarmsPass propositions become true (i.e., all self-test steps passed and alarms are confirmed working).

The translation G(SelfTestMode -> F(selfTestPass & testAlarmsPass)) captures that globally, whenever the system enters self-test mode, it is eventually the case that all self-test steps and alarms tests pass. Other properties, such as simply G(selfTestPass) or G(testAlarmsPass), would not specifically connect the self-test requirement to the context in which it is supposed to occur. This translation directly encodes the procedural guarantee the requirement implies, and uses only the available atomic propositions.

The natural language requirement specifies that the system must have a self-test procedure that verifies the system and its accessories are fully functional, and that alarms work. In the given atomic propositions, 'selfTestPass' represents successful completion of all self-test steps, while 'testAlarmsPass' indicates that local alarm tests have passed. To capture this requirement in LTL, we express that globally (always), if the system enters SelfTestMode, then eventually both the self-test and alarm test should pass (in any order, but both required). This ensures the self-test procedure completes successfully, including alarm functionality. No numerical comparison operation is necessary for this abstraction.

The natural language input specifies that the system must have a self-test procedure ensuring that both the system (including accessories) are fully functional and alarms work. From the provided list, the proposition 'selfTestPass' indicates successful completion of all self-test steps, and 'testAlarmsPass' indicates that the local alarms test has passed (i.e., alarms are functional). Thus, the property should capture that whenever the self-test passes, the alarms must also be proven to work (i.e., alarm test has passed) at that time. This can be expressed as: 'Globally, if selfTestPass then testAlarmsPass must also hold.' LTL: G (selfTestPass -> testAlarmsPass). This covers the requirement that system and alarms must both be tested and proven functional as a result of the self-test procedure. Alternative translations omitting explicit reference to alarm function ('testAlarmsPass') or failing to relate self-test with alarm check would be incomplete for this requirement.

The natural language requirement states that the system must have a self-test procedure that verifies that both the system and its accessories are fully functional, and that the alarms work. Using the provided atomic propositions, the most relevant are 'selfTestPass' (all self-test steps passed successfully) and 'testAlarmsPass' (local alarms test pass). To express that both the general self-test and the alarms test must pass, we can use the LTL operator 'G' (globally) to indicate this should be checked at any time the self-test procedure is performed. Since there is no direct proposition for accessories, we interpret that their function is included in 'selfTestPass'. Hence, the LTL property 'G(selfTestPass -> testAlarmsPass)' states that whenever the self-test passes, the alarm test must also pass, thus ensuring that the full system—including alarms—is verified. This captures the natural language by ensuring that self-testing implies alarm functionality too. Alternative specifications that simply state 'G selfTestPass' or 'G testAlarmsPass' would not ensure the dependency between the system's self-test and the alarm verification, and would miss the combined requirement.

The natural language specifies that the system must include a self-test procedure ensuring both the system itself and its accessories are fully functional and that alarms work. Among the atomic propositions, 'selfTestPass' best captures the successful completion of all self-test steps, which would include verifying functionality of the system, its accessories, and alarm mechanisms. Therefore, the global LTL property 'G F selfTestPass' asserts that, at all times, it is always true that eventually the self-test passes, which matches the requirement for a system self-test as an ongoing safety property. Previous (nonexistent) LTLs might miss the ongoing nature or focus on only part of the system checks.

The input specification requires a self-test procedure that ensures the system and its accessories are fully functional, and its alarms work. The natural representation in LTL, given the available atomic propositions, is to require that whenever the SelfTestMode is active, the selfTestPass property eventually holds (i.e., the self-test completes successfully), along with testAlarmsPass (i.e., the alarms test passes). Since we have no atomic proposition for specifically testing accessories, but SelfTestMode and selfTestPass give assurance for the overall self-test and testAlarmsPass for correct alarm functionality, we express that whenever the system enters SelfTestMode, it is guaranteed that eventually both selfTestPass and testAlarmsPass happen. This is enforced for all occurrences of SelfTestMode. Previous LTL properties might erroneously omit the test of alarms or fail to link the success conditions to the self-test procedure. This formula expresses both conditions after every self-test is triggered:     G (SelfTestMode -> F (selfTestPass & testAlarmsPass))

The natural language requirement requests a self-test procedure that ensures that the system and its accessories are fully functional and that alarms work. In the provided set of atomic propositions, 'selfTestPass' represents that all self-test steps passed successfully, and 'testAlarmsPass' represents the successful test of local alarms. Thus, the LTL property 'G (SelfTestMode -> (F selfTestPass & F testAlarmsPass))' specifies that whenever the system is in SelfTestMode, it is guaranteed that eventually the self-test passes and eventually the test for alarms passes. This adequately captures the intent that the system and alarms must be shown to be functional as part of the self-test startup check. This approach is preferred because it explicitly connects the entering of self test mode to the requirement of self-test and alarm verification passing, using the available atomic propositions and allowable temporal operators.
